FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study, 6469 children aged 9.9 years at baseline, followed for 4 years, enrolled across 21 U.S. research sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using quadratic-mixed effect modelling to investigate relationship between individual DM usage (social media, video games, television/videos) and brain development over 4 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media usage measured separately as IV (self-reported estimated time spent using social media); paired with neural outcomes (cortical surface area, striatum, cerebellum volumes). Video game time also measured separately as IV; both qualify as individual digital media variables paired with within-person neural trait-level variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative inferential results reported: regression coefficients for social media usage × time quadratic interactions with cerebellum volumes (β = 0.02, p = 0.02 linear term; β = -0.02, p < 0.001 quadratic); Table 2 shows coefficients for DM usage interactions with all three brain regions

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for SM ↔ brain outcome relationship reported throughout (coefficients, confidence intervals, p-values in Table 2)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but SM/digital media is IV in the analyses, not DV-only. The paper examines effects of DM usage on brain development, making SM/DM the independent variable. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper clearly measures individual SM and video game time as separate constructs and tests their associations with neural outcomes. Meets all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 10,410 eligible participants at Year 1 and Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using multivariable logistic regression to assess association between parental monitoring and family conflict at year 1 and subsequent cyberbullying victimization at year 2

FT_IC2.evidence: No qualifying smartphone or social media variable is measured as IV or DV in this paper. The outcome is cyberbullying victimization; predictors are parental monitoring and family conflict (family environment measures). Cyberbullying alone without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable does not meet FT-IC2 per criteria § 2.4.

notes: Paper studies family environment predictors of cyberbullying victimization but does not include SM/phone use as a measured variable. Fails FT-IC2 cascade.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data analyzed using structural equation modeling

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using structural equation modeling to examine direct and indirect effects of traumatic stress exposure on dual systems model constructs

FT_IC2.evidence: No smartphone or social media variable appears in this paper. Variables examined are: traumatic stress exposure (IV), deviant peer association (mediator), impulse control and sensation-seeking (outcomes). No SM/phone construct measured as IV or DV. Paper focuses on trauma, peer association, and dual systems model cognitive constructs without any digital media measurement.

notes: Paper is empirical ABCD research but does not measure or analyze any SM/phone variables. Fails FT-IC2 cascade on grounds of no qualifying SM/phone exposure.
